title: Recognizing the Dominican community's presence and contributions to Washington Heights and Inwood.
plain_summary: This resolution honors the Dominican community in two New York City neighborhoods called Washington Heights and Inwood. The Dominican people have lived in these areas for a very long time and have helped make them special places. They have started businesses, created art, and had many leaders who have made the neighborhoods better. The House of Representatives wants to create a special district to remember and celebrate all the good things the Dominican community has done for these neighborhoods.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for conside

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes the Dominican community's presence and contributions to Washington Heights and Inwood neighborhoods in New York City.

sections.title: Whereas Clauses
sections.text: In sixteen thirteen, Juan Rodriguez, a free Black man from La Espanola, was the first immigrant, first Latino, and first Dominican to live in what became New York City. This forever connected Dominican history with the city. Juan Rodriguez's family members settled throughout New York City, with the largest group living in Washington Heights and Inwood for many decades. From eighteen ninety-two to nineteen twenty-four, hundreds of thousands of Dominicans came to New York through Ellis Island. Many settled in or passed through Washington Heights, including the Brea and Lample families. Roumula A. Brea arrived in the United States on April twenty-third, nineteen fifteen. Nine years later, on December twenty-third, nineteen twenty-four, Mr. Brea became a naturalized United States citizen. At that time, Mr. Brea lived with his wife Julia Brea on eight thirty-eight Riverside Drive, between one hundred fifty-ninth and one hundred sixtieth Streets. Mr. Brea served in both World War One and World War Two. Federico Lample, a successful merchant, arrived in New York City in nineteen seventeen to join his wife Elizabeth Lample at five sixty-five West one hundred sixty-second Street. The Lample's son, Federico Lample Junior, joined them in nineteen twenty-one to continue his education but ended up staying permanently. By nineteen forty-seven, Federico Lample Junior had become a United States citizen, which at that time meant giving up his Dominican citizenship. Since the nineteen sixties, Washington Heights has been the cultural center and memory keeper for Dominicans in New York City and throughout the United States. For the past forty years, people of Dominican heritage have consistently made up the majority of Washington Heights residents compared to any other single national group. In Washington Heights and Inwood, Dominicans found a vibrant and diverse neighborhood that they wholeheartedly embraced. They put their own lasting marks on a neighborhood they transformed and made their own through their contributions and legacy. Dominicans have been an essential part of Washington Heights and Inwood, actively participating in neighborhood life including advocacy, art, culture, literature, sports, business, and government. Dominicans have helped make Washington Heights and Inwood into an entrepreneurial community that features vibrant cultural centers, strong nonprofit organizations, and a growing and diverse business sector. In the nineteen fifties, Casa Dominicana, located at thirty-eight twenty-five Broadway, was founded by Dominican activists as a meeting space for political activities to overthrow the dictatorial Trujillo government. Casa Dominicana regularly organized fundraising events to support the anti-Trujillo movement and other freedom causes in Latin America. People of notable standing, including Columbia University Professor Dr. Jesus de Galindez, gave speeches at Casa Dominicana. One day after class, Dr. Galindez, who had written a thesis condemning the Dominican Government, disappeared and is believed to have been kidnapped and murdered by Trujillo's supporters. The disappearance of Dr. Galindez is considered one of the most terrible and still unsolved episodes in the shared history of the Dominican Republic and the United States. The Centro Civico Cultural Dominicano, a nonprofit community organization founded in nineteen sixty-two, continues to build on the valuable contributions of Dominicans by engaging and empowering Dominican and Hispanic communities locally. In nineteen sixty-six, Club Juan Pablo Duarte was founded on Dominican Independence Day to educate the public about Dominican history. The organization remains active today and is now known as Instituto Duartiano. Centro Cultural Deportivo Dominicano, a social club founded in nineteen sixty-six, remains a longstanding pillar of the local community where the sound of traditional merengue tipico continues to expand the cultural roots of the Dominican immigrant experience. Dominican native Normandia Maldonado had a long history of cultural activism in Washington Heights. She founded, alongside other notable Dominicans, several prominent and long-lasting organizations including the Instituto Duartiano of the United States in nineteen sixty-six, the Centro Cultural Ballet Quisqueya in nineteen sixty-seven, and the Dominican Day Parade Incorporated in nineteen eighty-three. In nineteen seventy-nine, the pioneering Community Association of Progressive Dominicans was founded at thirty-nine forty Broadway to deliver services in a culturally appropriate manner to Dominicans and other Latinos who were facing economic hardship and faced language and cultural barriers to accessing aid. The Dominican Women's Development Center has been a vital resource to the Washington Heights and Inwood communities since nineteen eighty-eight, providing comprehensive services specifically to working class, poor women, and families. Alianza Dominicana, a leading Dominican organization in Washington Heights for over two decades, became an economic engine that provided jobs to over three hundred fifty individuals who worked on issues such as domestic violence, drug abuse, and other social problems. Alianza Dominicana also played a vital role in helping families of the Flight five eighty-seven tragedy secure the services they needed. Alianza Dominicana also created a Cultural Center that serves as a hub for local artists who enrich northern Manhattan with their art, including literature, performing, and visual programs, fostering the artistic development of the neighborhood's youth. Dominican cultural and educational activists organized to reverse inequities in public schools within the area and developed Gregorio Luperon High School for Science and Mathematics to meet the needs of immigrant Spanish-speaking teenagers and to create an inclusive learning environment. Six schools in the area have been named after historic Dominican figures: Intermediate School two eighteen Salome Urena, Public School one thirty-two Juan Pablo Duarte School, Gregorio Luperon High School for Science and Mathematics, Mirabal Sisters Campus, Professor Juan Bosch Public School, and Public School eight Luis Belliard. Twenty-one streets, avenues, boulevards, corners, and ways commemorate the Dominican figures and events that have moved the Dominican people forward. These include Juan Pablo Duarte Boulevard, Staff Sergeant Riayan Agusto Tejeda Street, Wilson Batista Corner, and many others. An array of small and medium-size businesses in Washington Heights and Inwood continue to be supported by Dominican entrepreneurs and loyal customers, creating a sense of a prosperous people within the entire neighborhood. Dyckman Street restaurants have witnessed a revival creatively mixing Dominican cuisine with cuisines of different cultures, such as Japanese and Italian. Local favorites such as eight zero nine Bar and Grill, Mamajuana, La Nueva Espana, and La Casa Del Mofongo continue to attract customers citywide to dine on the new Dominican cuisine as they visit the neighborhood. The National Supermarket Association and the Bodega Association of the United States, the two largest Dominican food-retail businesses, have a strong presence in Washington Heights and Inwood, providing a vital service while also creating jobs and contributing to the neighborhood economic progress. The National Supermarket Association distributes its scholarships, with awards from one thousand dollars to five thousand dollars, only to college students who live in the neighborhoods where National Supermarket Association and Bodega Association supermarkets are located. Professional associations such as Dominicans on Wall Street, the Association of Dominican-American Supervisors and Administrators, the New York Dominican Officers Organization, the Dominican Bar Association, the Dominican Medical Association, and the Dominican Medical-Dental Society highlight the contributions of professional Dominicans nationwide to the development of their communities. The Dominican community was heavily involved in the advocacy and restoration efforts to bring local parks back to life in the nineteen nineties from their state of neglect and vandalism, such as High Bridge Park, Inwood Hill Park, Riverside Park, and Fort Tryon Park. Dominican artists and artists born to immigrant Dominican parents in Washington Heights add to the dynamic blending of cultures that has historically represented the neighborhood's identity. From the late nineteen fifties to mid nineteen sixties, the Malagon Sisters, one of the first Dominican music acts to successfully cross over, lived in Washington Heights at six hundred West one hundred sixty-fourth Street, Apartment forty-seven. The Malagon trio contributed to the exposure of merengue, cha-cha-cha, and other Latin American genres. Their vocal and wide variety of instrumental talents earned them opportunities to perform at prestigious venues such as the Apollo Theater and on iconic platforms such as the Ed Sullivan Show. Milly Quezada, the Queen of Merengue, was raised and rose to stardom in Washington Heights with Milly y los Vecinos, a band she created with her siblings. Quezada arrived in the neighborhood at age eleven and lived at five hundred West one hundred seventy-first Street, and she attended George Washington High School. Dominican-American artists Lucia Hierro and M. Tony Peralta have both been recognized internationally for their thought-provoking work exploring the Dominican-American experience in areas of class, culture, identity, and gender. Dominican theater artist Mino Lora co-founded the People's Theater Project, which provides creative youth development programs for children growing up in the immigrant communities of upper Manhattan. Playwright Lin-Manuel Miranda created In the Heights, the Tony Award-winning musical set in Washington Heights. It tells the tale of immigrant Usnavi de la Vega who longs to return to his native Dominican Republic but ultimately stays because he, like so many Dominicans, has become part of a vibrant community and has made it his permanent home. Cayena Publications is a publishing company based in Washington Heights and founded by Mary Ely Pena-Gratereaux. It shines a light on the immigrant experience by supporting literary works that project a positive representation of Dominicans and other Latinos in the United States. Author and activist Angie Cruz is a native of Washington Heights whose books Soledad, Let It Rain Coffee, and Dominicana highlight the issues of working-class life, gender, and race in New York City. The Dominican Film Festival in New York showcases filmmakers from twenty-one countries including the Dominican Republic who reflect the city's diverse and multicultural population. Semana Dominicana en Estados Unidos (Dominican Week in the United States) has been celebrated for twenty-five years, highlighting the social and cultural ties between the Dominican Republic and the United States, in addition to economic, commerce, and academic collaboration. The Dominican Day Parade in New York City has celebrated the vibrant Dominican heritage through history, traditions, and music since its beginnings in nineteen eighty-two on Audubon Avenue. It now attracts thousands of people who gather to enjoy the celebration of Dominican culture on the Avenue of the Americas. Three-time Most Valuable Player Alex Rodriguez and four-time Major League Baseball All-Star Dellin Betances of the New York Yankees were born in Washington Heights. Manny Ramirez, the award-winning Most Valuable Player and twelve-time All-Star baseball player, moved from the Dominican Republic to New York City where he attended George Washington High School in Washington Heights and became a baseball star. Guillermo Linares became the first Dominican elected to public office in New York City in nineteen ninety-one and also served on President Bill Clinton's Advisory Commission on Educational Excellence for Hispanic Americans. Adriano Espaillat is the first Dominican immigrant to be elected to the New York State Assembly in nineteen ninety-six and the first formerly undocumented person elected to the United States Congress in twenty sixteen, representing New York's thirteenth Congressional District which is predominantly Dominican American. Rolando Acosta was elected as the first Dominican-American New York State Supreme Court Justice in New York County in two thousand two. Judge Faviola Soto, who was born and raised in Hamilton Heights, was appointed as the first Dominican judge in New York in nineteen ninety-three and was the first Hispanic to sit on the New York State Court of Claims in two thousand six. The Dominican Studies Institute of the City University of New York is working on a National Register of Historic Places application for submission to the National Park Service. This would create a noncontiguous cultural heritage district within sections of Washington Heights and Inwood to document, acknowledge, and honor the contributions of the Dominican community and ensure that the Dominican cultural legacy to the neighborhoods does not fade from memory but rather remains an essential component of their ongoing rich and dynamic history.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: Now, therefore, be it resolved that the House of Representatives supports the designation of a noncontiguous Dominican cultural heritage district under the supervision of the National Park Service to acknowledge and celebrate the contributions of the Dominican community to New York City, New York State, and the Nation. The House recognizes that the cultural legacy and historical contributions of the Dominican community enrich, enliven, and fill Washington Heights and Inwood. The House honors the long-standing history and rich contributions of Dominican-American elected officials, artists, small business and bodega owners, writers, and trailblazers. The House supports the efforts to recognize and preserve the history of the Dominican people in Washington Heights and Inwood, including through the designation of historic buildings and districts. The House promises to fight against the displacement, gentrification, uprooting of low and moderate-income residents, and other factors threatening the history of Washington Heights.
